decision.prompt: A major global crisis erupts, threatening to destabilize both humanitarian efforts and international markets. Your organization's unique position offers a critical opportunity to intervene.

options.text: Launch a bold, public moral campaign, leveraging the family's immense influence to shame aggressors and galvanize an immediate, values-driven global response, even if it risks financial repercussions.
Orchestrate a meticulous, behind-the-scenes diplomatic and economic intervention, carefully leveraging alliances and financial levers to stabilize the situation through systemic, long-term solutions.
Focus resources on bolstering internal resilience and humanitarian aid, maintaining the family's moral authority as an arbiter while avoiding direct political entanglement in the crisis's core.

prose: The tongue retains the dark, oil-slicked bitterness of equatorial cacao, a dense and waxen grease that coats the throat like the heavy incense of a Roman chapel. To share this imported luxury with the inner circle is to taste the complicated, blood-warm soil of missions they have conquered not with the bayonet, but with the ledger and the Latin grammar. There is a gritty residue of cane sugar and dried red pepper beneath the fat of the bean—a sharp, stinging reminder of the Atlantic shipping lanes and the brittle, over-stretched networks of credit that keep the family's rivals at bay. It is the flavor of an empire that has outgrown the crude geometry of borders, settling instead into the deep, sweet marrow of global dependency and moral sway.

Yet, it was the cold, iron-sharp draft of well-water from the family—drawn from the limestone depths of their old leasehold—that set the day—'s measure, cleansing the palate of all foreign indulgence. It tasted of wet slate, sheep-grease, and deep-burrowing roots, a cold shock that stung the throat with the memory of the wet earth from which {given_name} first learned the price of winter oats and the weight of a tenant—'s prayer. In its absolute lack of sweetness, there lived a terrible, clean truth—a reminder that before they were discussed in the banks of London and the ministries of Paris, they were merely people of the stone and the rain, sworn to an unyielding covenant with a hungry land.

Now, as the tallow gutters and spits in its brass socket, the mouth is dry, coated with the stale, metallic tang of coal-smoke and river-silt—the inevitable ash of a life spent in the second-class carriages of the iron horse and the damp bellies of the cross-channel steamers. It is the flavor of an exhaustion that has ceased to be physical, settling instead into a permanent spiritual climate. Through this generation—'s relentless movement, the family—'s reach has been consecrated, yet the network is fragile, held together by ink and the failing health of a few tired men. What remains on the lip is this dry, enduring salt—the flavor of a world bound by their design, and the heavy, unswallowed knowledge of what the next generation must carry.

The pale, dry vintage from the northern hills of France bites the tongue with a cold, yeast-rich fizz, its tiny bubbles popping like miniature victories against the palate. It is a sharp, expensive draft of triumph that tastes of signed deeds, secured harbor berths, and the smooth, hard-won displacement of the old Protestant merchants who once held the monopoly on the Atlantic trade.

choice.text: Reflect on the tangible successes and strategic victories that expanded the family's reach and influence, ensuring a stable future.

prose: The black tea is steeped dark as bog-water, leaving a harsh, tannic coat on the teeth that speaks of old turf hearths and unadorned duty. It is a parsimonious brew, free of sugar or milk, forcing a quiet reckoning with the raw, astringent truth of what has been sacrificed to keep the family—'s honor untarnished in the eyes of the Almighty.

choice.text: Consider the ethical impact of the family's actions, seeking reassurance that their global power remains firmly rooted in their founding principles.

decision.prompt: The heavy, velvet bitterness of the imported cacao still coats your palate, mingling with the dry scent of cedar and old leather. This wealth of the purse and the catechism must be anchored, but the quiet of the library demands to know who will inherit the ledger of {surname} and the faith of our fathers.

options.text: We must bind our blood to the very sea-lanes that carry this sweet cargo. I will take the daughter of the shipping syndicate to my bed, ensuring {given_name}'s blood lives on in heirs who will command both the pulpit and the harbor.

The bite of the pepper on my tongue is the call of the missions. I shall take no partner and leave no heirs of my own body, instead dedicating the entirety of the {family_name} coffers to the priesthood, letting our mortal line thin to a whisper for the glory of the Church.

This house is already quiet enough; I will not invite a stranger to share my bed. Instead, I will raise my sister's eldest child as my sole apprentice, teaching them the delicate balance of faith and ledger so they may inherit the {surname} estate without the mess of a new marriage.

prose: The scrubbed, copper-flat draft of the vent-line could never choke out the heavy, ancient rot of the turf briquette smoldering in the hearth-brazier of the {family_name} quarters. It rose as a thick, tallowy reek of bog-oak and waterlogged fern, smuggled across seventy millions of cold miles in oil-rubbed sheepskin to burn under the pressurized curve of the iron ceiling. Beneath its sweet, greasy smoke lay the stark, blue-white snap of the air-scrubbers—the sterile, lifeless breath of a machine that kept the absolute zero of the void from swallowing their tiny, huddled parish.

This was the air {given_name} drew in deep during the morning rosary, a sharp communion of the old world's decay and the high-frontier's clean, unyielding steel. Through the bulkhead seams drifted the cloying, chemical rot of the hydroponic bays—forced rosemary and the thin, sour dampness of pale clover struggling in crushed-basalt loam under the glare of low-sodium tubes. It was a quiet war of the senses, where the wet, wild stink of a rain-lashed county fought the grease-slicked, metallic logic of the vacuum, a bridge of gray smoke hung over the gulf of their generation-long exile.

To breathe here was to taste the marrow of their six-generation migration. For {full_name}, this draft carried the musk of an unswerving covenant: the scorched resin of solder from the chapel's brass air-pipes, the yellowed paper of calfskin missals, and the lard-fat sweetness of cheap candles. It was a frontier litany of soot, machine-grease, and holy water, an insistence that even out here in the dry, dead belly of an asteroid, the stone could be baptized until it remembered the names of their dead.

A warm, sour rising of soda bread swells from the coil-oven, its crust charring to a dark, floury gold that fills the steel partition with the starch-sweet scent of a county long since turned to dust—a {family_name} heritage stubbornly rebuilt in the cold marrow of the sky.

choice.text: Lean into the comfort of shared traditions, finding strength in the familiar.

prose: A sharp, icy backdraft from the lower drift brings the bitter, sulfurous stink of the deep-shaft laser bores—the raw, electric bite of pulverized iron and crushed quartz as the crew tears open the asteroid's heart, choking the domestic warmth in the scent of progress and sweat.

choice.text: Embrace the challenging advancements, understanding their necessity for future prosperity.

prose: The viewport’s reinforced glass was a slab of absolute, bone-reaping cold, a boundary thinner than a winter leaf between the blood-warmth of the {family_name} line and the black, unchristened void. When {given_name} pressed a hand against its curve, the pane did not merely resist; it bit with a salt-frost that sought the marrow, while beneath it ran the low, iron tremor of the primary reaction wheels. The entire pressurized hull thrummed like a steel lung, its shivering vibrations traveling through the soles of grease-stained boots—a constant, tactile reminder that they lived in a suspended tomb, breathing a dry, synthetic draft that smelled of scorched solder and recycled copper.

Kneeling brought a different, sharper ache: the coarse, punishing weave of the wool-and-linen prayer mat, its fibers stiffened by the salt-sweat of ancestral knees that had once pressed into the damp earth of a forgotten island. To drag one's palms across this rough, hand-loomed relic was to feel the grit of a soil long left behind, a deliberate, scouring friction against the station’s frictionless deck-plates. It was a tactile liturgy—clinging to the abrasive, oil-stained wool of the past to avoid slipping into the slick, chemical-scented oblivion of their new steel home.

Then came the sickening, weightless lurch as the habitat shifted its pitch, the gravity slackening until the stomach floated and the bones felt hollowed out. In the sudden dark, survival became an affair of white-knuckled friction: the greasy, desperate grip required to bolt a copper coupler, or the bruising pressure of skin against skin as hands locked together in the dark to anchor each other against the drift. It was a life lived on the narrow seam between the iron of the machine and the fragile grit of their own flesh.

The ancient rosary beads, carved from black bog oak and polished to a glass-like slickness by three generations of calloused thumbs, coiled like a cool, heavy snake in {given_name}’s hand, their solid, rhythmic weight providing the only true center of gravity in a world gone weightless.

choice.text: Cling to the tangible symbols of faith and heritage, finding stability in tradition.

prose: The glass-fronted terminal hummed beneath {given_name}’s fingertips, its slick, micro-vibrating surface yielding to the lightest brush of flesh with an almost electric warmth, promising a clean, frictionless future untethered from the heavy mud of legacy.

choice.text: Embrace the innovations of this new frontier, seeking efficiency and progress.

decision.prompt: The community faces a resource allocation challenge for a new orbital farm module. One faction advocates for prioritizing yield to ensure self-sufficiency, while another suggests allocating space for specialized, Earth-originating crops for cultural and spiritual significance, even at a lower overall output.

options.text: Support maximizing crop yield, arguing that sustenance and security must precede sentimental considerations in this new environment.
Advocate for allocating space to traditional crops, emphasizing the importance of spiritual and cultural nourishment for the community's well-being.

prose: The station’s cavernous ribcage groaned with the black, industrial thrum of the deep turbines, a shuddering iron vibration that lived in the marrow long before it reached the inner ear. Beneath the cold flagstones of the common market, the reactor hummed its endless, flat octave—the heavy, mechanical pulse that kept five generations of the family from spilling their lungs into the vacuum. Through this baseline cut the sharp, copper-dry cries of the water-mongers, the rapid-fire rattle of dock-slang traded in four different dialects of the low-rim, and the hard, rhythmic clack of the arrival boards tracking the iron-ore haulers from the deep trenches.

Yet, cutting through this mechanical gale like an oil-slick on grease, came the thin, salt-cured thread of an ancient Latin cadence. {full_name} caught the low, stubborn lift of the Kyrie, drifting from the raw-timber porch where the old parish memories were kept behind wax-sealed bulkheads. It was a fragile, human rasping against the engine's throat; it fought for air against the wet, syncopated slap of grease-drums from the lower holds—the heathen rhythms of the second-wave crews—and the relentless, high-frequency hiss of the news-casters spitting out the latest blood-debts from the outer sectors.

In the private quarters behind the chapel, the noise of the void narrowed to the dry, rhythmic scrape of the brass-headed sweepers clearing the gray dust of the filter-beds—a domestic tick-tock that mimicked the grandfather clocks of West Cork. It was a thin shelter of sound, though it never quite drowned the whistle of the oxygen scrubbing lines or the sudden, structural pop of iron joists settling as the station rolled into planetary shadow. Here, one lived in the jaws of three distinct noises: the predatory roar of the mining syndicates, the low, whispered rosaries of their own bloodline, and the hard, clanging tongues of those who would see the {family_name} name erased from the registry.

A sudden, high-pitched shriek tore through the secondary pipes—the three-stroke howl of a pressure drop that set the copper joints to screaming. Through the stampede of iron-shod boots on the grating, {given_name} heard the slow, heavy toll of the chapel bell, a measured iron tongue striking to summon the parish to the nave before the pressure bulkheads dropped.

choice.text: Seek out the community elders, trusting their practiced leadership in uncertain times.

prose: Then came the massive, rib-cracking shudder of a foreign stabilizer, an ore-hauler from the rival syndicate groaning as its magnetic clamps slammed home against the station's skin. The roar was deep and greasy, rattling the heavy pewter mugs on the shelf and bringing the high-pitched whine of the cranes unloading raw-cut slag, punctuated by the harsh, foreign-tongued shouts of dockers who had run the blockade.

choice.text: Investigate the rival's cargo and crew movements, looking for potential leverage or alliance opportunities.

thread.crossing: The sour, hot scent of scorched copper sealant hung in the dry air as the Italian syndicate lead, {surname}, slid a heavy, brass-faced ledger across the iron crate, offering a lock on the reactor-shielding contracts. Your mother, representing the {family_name} interest, let her fingers rest on the vibrating guard-rail, refusing to touch the stylus while she knew {given_name}’s grease-monkeys still held the wrench-keys to the upper sector's main line. It was a silent, hard-eyed calculation over the station's throat; it would either lift their houses into a shared empire or freeze them both in the dark.

prose: The viewport did not merely frame the void; it bent the light of five hundred dying stars through lead-tempered glass, throwing cold indigo and amethyst bruises across the brow of {full_name}. Out there, the unbaptized planet hung like an icy turquoise bead suspended in a well of ink, its atmosphere still choked by the greasy grey smudges of sulfur-burns. It was a terrible, unpeopled vastness, a wilderness of raw hydrogen and silent stone that demanded either a sanctuary or a strip-mine, offering no middle ground to the eyes of those whose ancestors had spent six generations sifting cold muck for a foothold.

Inside, the deck’s air was thick with the greenish glare of tactical vectors and population charts, casting flickering grids across the assembled council. One could see the divide in the very tilt of their faces: the catechists, their eyes wide and glassy with the reflection of the new world’s pristine valleys; the engineers, squinting at the orange-lined specs of heavy-water silos; and the younger sons of the {family_name} line, whose gazes lingered on the high-tonnage docking manifests of the blockade-runners. Every shadow in the room was long, stretched thin by the low-wattage emergency lamps of a vessel that had traveled too far from its original sun to waste light on comfort.

On the master-map, the copper-gilded icon of the winged serpent—the mark of their rivals—burned with a sharp, predatory lime-green, its tail hooked aggressively around the salt-rich moons of the outer belt. Against that sharp, hungry needle-point, the old, rounded curves of the {family_name} crest looked heavy, agrarian, and dangerously soft to the uninitiated eye. To look upon those overlapping territories was to see a border waiting to be bled; the choice before them would either sharpen their boundaries into iron teeth or expand their fold to swallow the faithless whole.
The distant nebula boiled in shades of church-candle gold and pale violet, its light washing over {given_name}’s face like the glow of an ancient tabernacle left open to the dark.

choice.text: Contemplate the spiritual significance of the cosmos, seeing it as God's new frontier.

prose: Through the lower ports, the black silhouette of the processing plant cut a jagged, toothy line against the stars, its fuel flares spitting bright, violent orange sparks that spoke of immediate, hard-won profit.

choice.text: Focus on the tangible assets and strategic importance of their current holdings.

decision.prompt: The {family_name} line holds significant influence in the nascent stellar collective. A proposal is put forth for the expansion of human presence to a promising exoplanet. The choice before you is how your line will shape this new colonization effort, balancing ambition with your enduring values.

options.text: Champion a strictly faith-based colonization model, creating a 'New Éire' of spiritual purity and communal self-sufficiency, separate from the broader collective's secular interests.
Lead the charge for an inclusive, cooperative colonization, integrating {family_name} values of community and perseverance into a diverse collective, seeking to uplift all settlers.

Advocate for a pragmatic, resource-driven approach to colonization, focusing on economic opportunity and strategic positioning, even if it means de-emphasizing some traditional aspects of the {family_name} heritage.

prose: The host dissolved upon the tongue with the dry, alkaline sting of reclaimed moisture, leaving behind a lingering chalkiness that tasted of recycled lime and iron-rich dust. It was the flavor of a sterile miracle: potato-starch forced from aerated pumice in the black of the fifth tier, thousands of leagues from the rain-lashed ditches where their ancestors once dug for survival. Beneath the chemical flatness of the wafer lay the faint, bitter memory of the soil itself—a stubborn, engineered grit that {full_name} swallowed with the quiet piety of a family that had survived three famines on Earth and two migrations through the cold stars. To swallow this dry bread of the void was to take the oath of the line: a refusal to let the blood turn thin, even when the only water was reclaimed from their own sweat.

Then came the drop from the flask, a chipped earthenware vessel that had crossed the gulf of the skies in the chests of their grandfathers. There was no sweet smell of peat-reek or river-mud in this clean, engineered vapor, but the liquor itself bit the throat with a raw, blistering heat that tasted of scorched grain and zinc-sheathed copper. {given_name} held the cup with hands toughened by years of tightening pressure valves, passing the heavy vessel to the heir as if it held the very fat of the home parish. It scorched the gums, leaving a sharp, medicinal burn behind the teeth—a fierce, distilled memory of elderberry and toasted barley that survived the frost of the transit to warm the marrow of those born in the dark.

Long after the vessel was dried, the mouth retained the iron-penny tang of the station’s recycled breath and the sulfurous bite of their daily rations. Yet beneath the sterile salt and the zinc lay a deep, greasy richness—the phantom taste of fat mutton and parched oats that had sustained their name through six generations of exile. It was the flavor of an enclave refusing to turn to ash in the cold iron of this new world, proving that even here, where the sun was nothing but a distant spark through a reinforced port, the tongue could still summon the rich, heavy grease of their own earth.

The heir, {heir_given_name}, tore a heel of the dense, grey soda loaf—heavy with the bitter rising-agents of the air-scrubbers and the dry, parched grain of the hydroponic shelves—chewing with a deliberate slowness that made a sacrament of their hunger.

choice.text: Reinforce the importance of preserving cultural and culinary traditions for future generations.

prose: The heir, {heir_given_name}, took down a salt-cured strip of the high-yield protein slab, its texture like old boot-leather and its flavor sharp with the cold iron of the station's newest converters—the taste of a hard, unyielding discipline that would outlast the steel of their cage.

choice.text: Emphasize the practical advancements and resourcefulness gained this generation, key to the line's future.

decision.prompt: As the artificial humidity of the chapel settles like dew on your brow, the taste of the chemical Host lingers on your tongue. How will {full_name} ensure the spirit of the {family_name} survival outlasts this metal cage?

options.text: I will kneel before the chaplain alongside the daughter of the hydroponics overseer, combining our meager water allotments to raise children who will know the old prayers and tend the synthetic soil.
My current vow of solitary contemplation is my shield; instead, I will adopt my late cousin's orphaned ward, teaching him how to nurse life from aerated loam without the burden of a marriage bed.

This dead metal cannot hold a living line. I will take holy orders, offering my flesh entirely to the upkeep of the station's silent chapel, letting the {family_name} name dissolve into the holy peace of the void.
